component.name: Accordion
component.description: A vertically stacked set of disclosure panels. Each item has a trigger (title + optional subtitle) and a collapsible content panel. Multiple items can be open simultaneously. Follows the WAI-ARIA Accordion pattern.

when: Presenting a list of content sections where users may only need to read a subset (e.g. course curriculum, FAQ, settings groups)
Reducing vertical space by hiding detail until requested
The content within each panel is self-contained and does not need to be visible simultaneously

patterns.name: course-curriculum
patterns.description: A list of course modules, each expandable to reveal a description.

patterns.name: faq-list
patterns.description: A set of frequently asked questions where each answer is hidden by default.

antiPatterns.scenario: Using Accordion for primary navigation or tabbed content
antiPatterns.reason: Accordion is a disclosure pattern, not navigation. Tabs or a nav component are the correct choice when content is mutually exclusive or linked to URLs.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use a Tabs component or page-level navigation

antiPatterns.reason: Deep nesting creates a confusing mental model and breaks the heading hierarchy.
antiPatterns.alternative: Flatten the information architecture or use a sub-section heading and Text

antiPatterns.scenario: Placing an Accordion where all items are always open
antiPatterns.reason: If the content is always visible, the disclosure mechanism adds interaction cost without benefit.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use Stack with Heading and Text for always-visible sections

antiPatterns.scenario: Rendering the subtitle prop as a raw string, span, or plain HTML element
antiPatterns.reason: Raw elements bypass the design system's typography scale and color tokens.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use <Text as='span' size='metadata' color='subtle'> for the subtitle prop.

antiPatterns.scenario: Setting a color prop or style directly on the chevron Icon
antiPatterns.reason: Icon uses currentColor — setting color on the Icon itself bypasses the token system.
antiPatterns.alternative: Apply color.icon.default on the trigger button element; the Icon inherits it via currentColor.

antiPatterns.scenario: Conditionally rendering the panel element (e.g. {isOpen && <div>}) instead of always mounting it
antiPatterns.reason: aria-controls on the trigger must point to an element that is always in the DOM. Unmounting the panel when closed means the target id does not exist in the collapsed state, breaking the ARIA association for screen readers such as NVDA + Firefox.
antiPatterns.alternative: Always render the panel div. The native hidden attribute cannot be used directly because it forces display:none, which would break the height-transition animation — instead pair aria-hidden={!isOpen} with the panel element's inert DOM property (set imperatively via a ref, since @types/react 18 doesn't expose inert as a JSX prop) so focusable descendants inside a collapsed panel cannot be tabbed into or clicked while it is visually collapsing.

antiPatterns.scenario: Passing both the open prop (controlled) and defaultOpen prop simultaneously to AccordionItem
antiPatterns.reason: The component cannot resolve two competing sources of truth. The controlled open prop silently wins over defaultOpen; a DEV-only console.warn is emitted, but the initial state discrepancy still occurs at runtime.
antiPatterns.alternative: Use open + onOpenChange for fully controlled mode, or defaultOpen alone for uncontrolled mode. Never supply both.

antiPatterns.scenario: Placing elements other than AccordionItem as direct children of the Accordion container
antiPatterns.reason: The Accordion container's CSS and ARIA structure assumes all direct children are AccordionItem elements. Arbitrary children break the border-divider pattern and the heading hierarchy.
antiPatterns.alternative: All direct children of Accordion must be AccordionItem. Place other content above or below the Accordion wrapper.

purpose.default: Single variant. Visual style is always the same: transparent trigger, elevated background when expanded.

states.description: Panel visible with elevated background; trigger shows chevron-up.

accessibility.notes: Each AccordionItem trigger is a <button> wrapped in a heading element (default h3, configurable via headingLevel). The panel uses role='region' with aria-labelledby pointing to the trigger. The Accordion container itself has no ARIA role — it is a plain div. The chevron Icon inside each trigger must carry aria-hidden="true" — without it, the Icon's implicit accessible name contributes to the button's name string, which screen readers may announce unexpectedly.
ariaAttributes: aria-controls (trigger → panel id) — the panel element must always be present in the DOM; use hidden={!isOpen} rather than conditional rendering so this id always resolves

keyboardInteractions.action: Toggles the focused accordion item open or closed
Toggles the focused accordion item open or closed

Moves focus sequentially through any focusable content within an open panel, then to the next accordion trigger. Focusable descendants inside a collapsed panel are skipped — the panel's inert DOM property is set while closed, so Tab cannot land on them even though they remain in the DOM for aria-controls.
